title: Chinese Hot and Sour Soup

ingredients.name: firm tofu, cut into thin matchsticks

subs.name: paneer, cut into thin strips
subs.notes: paneer holds its shape well in the soup; cut it as thin as the tofu would be

ingredients.name: button or shiitake mushrooms, sliced thin

subs.name: thinly sliced carrot ribbons or bamboo shoot strips
subs.notes: any crunchy vegetable works as a textural element

ingredients.name: rice or white vinegar
visual_checks: start with 3 tbsp and add more to taste — the soup should be noticeably sour
ingredients.notes: start with 3 tbsp and add more to taste — the soup should be noticeably sour

subs.name: lemon juice at the end
subs.notes: add lemon juice off heat to preserve brightness; vinegar cooks differently

ingredients.name: white pepper (or black pepper)
visual_checks: white pepper is the traditional choice and gives a different heat from black; add generously
ingredients.notes: white pepper is the traditional choice and gives a different heat from black; add generously

ingredients.name: cornflour, mixed with 4 tbsp cold water

subs.name: water with 2 stock cubes + 1 tsp soy sauce
subs.notes: a good stock makes the soup dramatically better

steps.text: Bring the stock to a simmer in a large pot with the ginger slices. Add soy sauce, vinegar and pepper. Taste and adjust — the broth should be distinctly sour and peppery before you add the other ingredients.
Add the mushrooms and simmer for 3 minutes until softened.
Gently add the tofu matchsticks. Simmer for 2 minutes — tofu will warm through without losing its shape.

Give the cornflour slurry a quick stir and pour it slowly into the simmering soup while stirring in a circular motion. The soup will thicken noticeably within 1 minute. Stop adding slurry once it reaches a light, silky consistency.

Reduce the heat to low. Beat the eggs well, then pour them in a thin, steady stream while stirring the soup gently in one direction — this creates the characteristic egg ribbons. Stop stirring after 30 seconds and let the egg set.
Remove the ginger slices. Taste and add more vinegar, pepper or salt as needed. Drizzle with sesame oil, garnish with spring onions and serve piping hot.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tofu → paneer, cut into thin strips (paneer holds its shape well in the soup; cut it as thin as the tofu would be); mushroom → thinly sliced carrot ribbons or bamboo shoot strips (any crunchy vegetable works as a textural element); stock → water with 2 stock cubes + 1 tsp soy sauce (a good stock makes the soup dramatically better); vinegar → lemon juice at the end (add lemon juice off heat to preserve brightness; vinegar cooks differently)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Bean curd (tofu) is available in Indian supermarkets and Chinese grocery shops; paneer is a local alternative. Button mushrooms are widely available. White vinegar (sirka) is your souring agent — start conservatively and add more. Cornflour is available in all Indian supermarkets. Use a good stock cube for depth. The whole soup can be made in a kadai or heavy pot in 20 minutes.
